```{r simple-slopes-functions}
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      # t-statistic and p-value
      t_stat <- slope / slope_se
      p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - length(coefs), lower.tail = FALSE)
      
      # Add to results
      results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
        player_type = pt,
        estimate = slope,
        std.error = slope_se,
        statistic = t_stat,
        p.value = p_val,
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      ))
    }
  }
  
  # Add confidence intervals and formatting
  results <- results %>%
    mutate(
      outcome = outcome_name,
      conf.low = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
      conf.high = estimate + 1.96 * std.error,
      significant = p.value < 0.05,
      stars = case_when(
        p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
        p.value < 0.01 ~ "**",
        p.value < 0.05 ~ "*",
        p.value < 0.1 ~ "†",
        TRUE ~ ""
      )
    )
  
  return(results)
}

# Function to create interaction plots for all player types
create_interaction_plots <- function(model, outcome_name) {
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a grid of values for hours
  hours_range <- range(pvz_df_clustered$hours, na.rm = TRUE)
  hours_grid <- seq(from = hours_range[1], to = hours_range[2], length.out = 100)
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  
  # Create a dataframe to store predictions
  predictions <- data.frame()
  
  # For each player type, calculate predicted values across hours range
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    # Calculate intercept
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"]
      slope <- hours_coef
    } else {
      # Other levels
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"] + fixed_effects[paste0("player_type", pt)]
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
    }
    
    # Calculate predictions
    pred <- intercept + slope * hours_grid
    
    # Add to predictions dataframe
    predictions <- rbind(predictions, data.frame(
      player_type = pt,
      hours = hours_grid,
      predicted = pred
    ))
  }
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(predictions, aes(x = hours, y = predicted, color = player_type)) +
    geom_line(size = 1.2) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = predicted - 1.96 * 0.5, ymax = predicted + 1.96 * 0.5, fill = player_type), alpha = 0.2) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Interaction Plot for", outcome_name),
      x = "Hours Played",
      y = outcome_name,
      color = "Player Type",
      fill = "Player Type"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")
  
  return(p)
}

# Function to create Johnson-Neyman plot
create_jn_plot <- function(model, moderator = "player_type", predictor = "hours", outcome_name) {
  # For rlmerMod models, we'll use a custom approach
  # This function creates a simplified version of Johnson-Neyman plot
  
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  vcov_matrix <- vcov(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a dataframe to store slopes and confidence intervals
  jn_data <- data.frame(
    player_type = player_types,
    slope = numeric(length(player_types)),
    se = numeric(length(player_types)),
    lower_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    upper_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  hours_se <- sqrt(vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"])
  
  # For each player type, calculate slope and CI
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      jn_data$slope[i] <- hours_coef
      jn_data$se[i] <- hours_se
    } else {
      # Other levels
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
      
      # Calculate SE using delta method (simplified)
      var_hours <- vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"]
      var_inter <- vcov_matrix[interaction_term, interaction_term]
      cov_hours_inter <- vcov_matrix["hours", interaction_term]
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      jn_data$slope[i] <- slope
      jn_data$se[i] <- slope_se
    }
    
    # Calculate 95% confidence intervals
    jn_data$lower_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] - 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
    jn_data$upper_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] + 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
  }
  
  # Determine significance
  jn_data$significant <- (jn_data$lower_ci > 0) | (jn_data$upper_ci < 0)
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(jn_data, aes(x = player_type, y = slope, color = significant)) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower_ci, ymax = upper_ci), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Conditional Effect of", predictor, "on", outcome_name),
      subtitle = "by Player Type with 95% Confidence Intervals",
      x = "Player Type",
      y = paste("Effect of", predictor),
      color = "Significant"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("gray50", "blue"))
  
  return(p)
}
```

```{r wellbeing-simple-slopes}
#| label: wellbeing-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Calculate simple slopes for game-specific well-being model
wellbeing_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_game, "Game-Specific Well-being")

# Create a formatted table for game-specific well-being simple slopes
wellbeing_slopes_table <- wellbeing_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      TRUE ~ round(p.value, 3) %>% as.character()
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(player_type, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, ci, stars)

kable(wellbeing_slopes_table, 
      caption = "Simple Slopes Analysis for Game-Specific Well-being",
      col.names = c("Player Type", "Estimate", "SE", "t-value", "p-value", "95% CI", ""),
      align = "lrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

```{r enjoyment-simple-slopes}
#| label: enjoyment-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Calculate simple slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "Intrinsic Enjoyment")

# Create a formatted table for enjoyment simple slopes
enjoyment_slopes_table <- enjoyment_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      TRUE ~ round(p.value, 3) %>% as.character()
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(player_type, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, ci, stars)

kable(enjoyment_slopes_table, 
      caption = "Simple Slopes Analysis for Intrinsic Enjoyment",
      col.names = c("Player Type", "Estimate", "SE", "t-value", "p-value", "95% CI", ""),
      align = "lrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

```{r wellbeing-pairwise-comparisons}
#| label: wellbeing-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for game-specific well-being
wellbeing_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                        player_type2 = character(),
                        difference = numeric(),
                        std.error = numeric(),
                        statistic = numeric(),
                        p.value = numeric(),
                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    wellbeing_pairs <- rbind(wellbeing_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for subjective well-being
wellbeing_pairs <- wellbeing_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    stars = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "**",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "*", 
      p.value < 0.1 ~ "†",
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      TRUE ~ round(p.value, 3) %>% as.character()
    )
  )

kable(wellbeing_pairs, 
      caption = "Pairwise Comparison of Simple Slopes for Game-Specific Well-being",
      col.names = c("Player Type 1", "Player Type 2", "Difference", "SE", "t-value", "p-value", ""),
      align = "llrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

```{r enjoyment-pairwise-comparisons}
#| label: enjoyment-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                              player_type2 = character(),
                              difference = numeric(),
                              std.error = numeric(),
                              statistic = numeric(),
                              p.value = numeric(),
                              stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    enjoyment_pairs <- rbind(enjoyment_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- enjoyment_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    stars = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "**",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "*", 
      p.value < 0.1 ~ "†",
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      TRUE ~ round(p.value, 3) %>% as.character()
    )
  )

kable(enjoyment_pairs, 
      caption = "Pairwise Comparison of Simple Slopes for Enjoyment",
      col.names = c("Player Type 1", "Player Type 2", "Difference", "SE", "t-value", "p-value", ""),
      align = "llrrrrl") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

## Interaction Plots

```{r interaction-plots}
#| label: interaction-plots
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 6
#| fig.cap: "Interaction plots showing the relationship between hours played and outcomes across player types"

# Create scatter plots for total playtime vs. outcomes across player types with APA-style formatting
wellbeing_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-6, 6), breaks = seq(-6, 6, 2)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "SPANE Score",
    color = "Player Type"
  ) +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  )

enjoyment_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = enjoyment, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7), breaks = seq(1, 7, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "Enjoyment Score",
    color = "Player Type"
  ) +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  )

# Combine the plots with common legend at bottom
ggarrange(wellbeing_plot, enjoyment_plot, ncol = 2, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "bottom")
```

## Johnson-Neyman Plots

```{r johnson-neyman-plots}
#| label: johnson-neyman-plots
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig.width: 10
#| fig.height: 6
#| fig.cap: "Johnson-Neyman plots showing conditional effects of hours played by player type on Game-Specific Well-being and Enjoyment"

# Load patchwork if not already loaded
if (!requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(patchwork)
}

# Create Johnson-Neyman plots with consistent formatting following APA standards
jn_wellbeing <- create_jn_plot(rmodel_moderation_game, outcome_name = "Game-Specific Well-being") +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  ) +
  # Modify labels to be more concise for combined plot
  labs(
    title = "A: Game-Specific Well-being",
    subtitle = NULL,
    y = "Effect of Hours Played"
  )

jn_enjoyment <- create_jn_plot(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, outcome_name = "Enjoyment") +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  ) +
  # Modify labels to be more concise for combined plot
  labs(
    title = "B: Enjoyment",
    subtitle = NULL,
    y = "Effect of Hours Played"
  )

# Combine the plots with shared legend at the bottom
combined_plot <- jn_wellbeing + jn_enjoyment + 
  plot_layout(ncol = 2, guides = "collect") & 
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 9))

# Display the combined plot
combined_plot
```

To further understand the moderation effects, we conducted simple slopes analyses to examine how the relationship between hours played and well-being outcomes varied across the three player types.

For game-specific well-being, hours played had a significant positive association for Casual PVE Players (b = 0.142, SE = 0.042, p \< .001, 95% CI \[0.059, 0.225\]), indicating that increased playtime was associated with higher well-being for this player type. For Casual PVP Players, hours played did not significantly predict well-being (b = -0.032, SE = 0.048, p = .505, 95% CI \[-0.126, 0.062\]), suggesting that playtime had no reliable effect on well-being for these players. For Elite Allround Players, hours played showed a significant positive relationship with well-being (b = 0.126, SE = 0.053, p = .018, 95% CI \[0.022, 0.230\]), similar to the pattern observed for Casual PVE Players.

Pairwise comparisons of these slopes revealed significant differences between player types (see Appendix 1). The relationship between hours played and well-being was significantly stronger for Casual PVE Players compared to Casual PVP Players (difference = 0.174, SE = 0.064, p \< .001). Similarly, the slope for Elite Allround Players was significantly stronger than for Casual PVP Players (difference = 0.158, SE = 0.071, p = .027). No significant difference was found between the slopes for Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players (difference = 0.016, SE = 0.068, p = .815).

For enjoyment, a similar pattern emerged. Casual PVE Players showed a significant positive relationship between hours played and enjoyment (b = 0.108, SE = 0.033, p = .001, 95% CI \[0.043, 0.173\]). For Casual PVP Players, no significant relationship was found (b = -0.045, SE = 0.038, p = .237, 95% CI \[-0.120, 0.030\]). Elite Allround Players showed a significant positive relationship between hours played and enjoyment (b = 0.086, SE = 0.042, p = .041, 95% CI \[0.004, 0.168\]), though the magnitude was slightly smaller than for Casual PVE Players.

Pairwise comparisons of these slopes also revealed significant differences (see Appendix 1). The relationship between hours played and enjoyment was significantly stronger for Casual PVE Players compared to Casual PVP Players (difference = 0.153, SE = 0.050, p = .002). Similarly, the slope for Elite Allround Players was significantly stronger than for Casual PVP Players (difference = 0.131, SE = 0.057, p = .021). No significant difference was found between the slopes for Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players (difference = 0.022, SE = 0.053, p = .679).

### Visualization of Interaction Effects

The interaction plots (Figure 6) visually confirmed these differential relationships, showing positive slopes for Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players, and flat or slightly negative slopes for Casual PVP Players for both outcome variables. These visualizations highlighted that the relationship between playtime and well-being outcomes was moderated by player type.

The Johnson-Neyman plots (Figure 7) further illustrated these conditional effects by displaying the estimated effect of hours played on each outcome variable across player types, with confidence intervals. These plots confirmed that the effect of hours played was significantly different from zero for Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players, but not for Casual PVP Players.

The heatmap visualizations of pairwise differences in simple slopes (Appendix 1) provided an additional perspective on these findings. The heatmaps highlighted the significant differences between Casual PVP Players and the other two player types, with color gradients indicating the magnitude and direction of these differences.

# Discussion

The aims of this study were two-fold. First, to challenge the assumption of homogeneity amongst players by demonstrating the existence of distinct playing styles based on behavioral telemetry. Second, to highlight the importance of accounting for player-specific playing style differences in analyzing the psychosocial outcomes of video game play. Taken as a whole, these results demonstrate that even players of the same game do not have a homogenous playing style and that subgroups with distinct playing styles can be clearly identified by clustering in-game behavioral telemetry data. We also found a slight positive relationship between overall active playtime and well-being, confirming the findings of previous studies [@johannesVideoGamePlay2021]. Furthermore, we found that playing styles are related to well-being and that Casual PVE Players reported significantly higher well-being scores compared to Casual PVP Players. Most importantly, significant moderation effects for player types were found, demonstrating that the relationship between playtime and well-being varies according to a player's playing style.

Through PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids) clustering of behavioral telemetry data, three distinct playing styles were identified. The Casual PVE Players (who preferred cooperative gameplay against AI opponents), Elite Allround Players (who demonstrated high performance across both PVP and PVE contexts), and Casual PVP Players (who favored competitive gameplay but exhibited only moderate performance). These clusters are conceptually similar to player segments previously identified in other shooter games [@drachenGunsSwordsData2012; @melhartYourGameplaySays2019]. For instance, @drachenGunsSwordsData2012 identified player types in Battlefield 2: Bad Company 2 that parallel our Casual Players and Elite players. Similarly, @melhartYourGameplaySays2019 identified comparable profiles in Tom Clancy's The Division, including players focused on PVE content and those excelling in competitive play. The consistency of these patterns across different games suggests that player typologies may represent fundamental approaches to gameplay rather than game-specific behaviors. These findings challenge the often-made assumption of homogeneous play behavior and instead show that it is an uninformative oversimplification. This finding suggests that there is no one prototypical player and that generalizing over a player base of even a single game has very little ecological validity.

Our robust linear mixed-effects models revealed significant differences in well-being and enjoyment across player types. Compared to Casual PVE Players, Casual PVP Players reported significantly lower well-being scores (b = -0.76, SE = 0.17, t = -4.39) and enjoyment levels (b = -0.72, SE = 0.13, t = -5.39). Elite Allround Players showed no significant differences from Casual PVE Players on either outcome measure. These findings indicate that different styles of engagement with the same game are associated with different psychological experiences, highlighting the importance of considering playing style as a factor in gaming research.

More importantly, our interaction models demonstrated that player type significantly moderated the relationship between playtime and psychological outcomes. For Casual PVE Players, hours played showed a significant positive association with both well-being (b = 0.14, SE = 0.04, t = 3.36) and enjoyment (b = 0.11, SE = 0.03, t = 3.24). For Elite Allround Players, we observed similar positive relationships between hours played and both well-being (b = 0.13, SE = 0.05, t = 2.38) and enjoyment (b = 0.09, SE = 0.04, t = 2.05). However, for Casual PVP Players, hours played showed no significant relationship with either well-being (b = -0.03, SE = 0.05, t = -0.67) or enjoyment (b = -0.05, SE = 0.04, t = -1.18).

The simple slopes analyses further clarified these differential relationships. The effect of playtime on well-being and enjoyment was significantly stronger for both Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players compared to Casual PVP Players, with no significant difference between Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players. This pattern suggests that the psychological benefits of increased playtime may be contingent on how players engage with the game. Those who excel across different gameplay contexts (Elite Allround Players) or focus on cooperative gameplay (Casual PVE Players) appear to derive greater psychological benefits from increased playtime compared to those who prefer competitive play but perform moderately (Casual PVP Players).

These findings have important implications for the playtime paradigm that has dominated video game effects research. They demonstrate that the psychosocial outcomes of playing video games cannot be attributed to playtime alone without considering playing styles. The relationship between playtime and well-being is not uniform across all players but varies significantly based on how players engage with the game. This may help explain inconsistencies in previous research, where studies focusing on different player populations might have captured different segments of these varied relationships.

The stronger relationship between playtime and well-being for Elite Allround Players and Casual PVE Players might be explained through the lens of need satisfaction theory. Previous work has shown that player performance in video games positively affects psychological competence [@klimmt2009a; @przybylskiMotivationalModelVideo2010; @tamboriniMediaEnjoymentNeed2011], and competence is a strong predictor of increased subjective well-being [@ryanHappinessHumanPotentials2001]. Elite Allround Players, who demonstrate high skill levels across gameplay contexts, likely experience greater competence satisfaction during play. Similarly, Casual PVE Players may derive satisfaction from mastering cooperative challenges without the potentially frustrating competition against other skilled players that characterizes PVP gameplay.

## Limitations and Future Directions

While the study provided valuable insights, we must acknowledge several limitations. First, the results should be interpreted within the context of this specific game, player population, and timeframe. We lack evidence that playing styles remain consistent across different games or time periods. Unlike personality traits, playing styles may be contextual and fluid, varying based on the game environment and a player's development over time. Second, while our behavioral telemetry provided comprehensive metrics on core gameplay mechanics and performance, it captured only a subset of potential player behaviors. Notably absent were metrics on social interactions, an important aspect of multiplayer gaming experiences. Third, the cross-sectional nature of our data limits causal inferences about the relationship between playing styles, playtime, and well-being.

Future research should address these limitations by examining the stability of playing styles across different games and over time, incorporating metrics on social interactions and other qualitative aspects of gameplay, and employing longitudinal or experimental designs to clarify causal relationships. Additionally, investigating the mechanisms through which different playing styles might influence well-being would be valuable. For instance, studies could directly measure need satisfaction (competence, autonomy, and relatedness) across different player types to test whether these mediate the relationship between playing style and well-being outcomes.

## Conclusion

The rise of video games to become the dominant form of entertainment across the globe has raised important questions about the potential benefits and costs of digital play. Understanding how and to what extent playing video games influences the well-being of its players concerns players, caregivers, game developers, and policymakers alike. To date, the literature has mostly followed a playtime paradigm, measuring and recommending how specific limits on playtime might minimize negative effects on players. However, our findings suggest that relying on playtime alone is a misguided oversimplification. This study demonstrates that player types, derived from actual in-game behavioral data, significantly moderate the relationship between playtime and well-being outcomes. The same amount of playtime may be associated with different psychological outcomes depending on how players engage with the game. These findings call for a more nuanced approach to understanding the effects of gaming on well-being, one that considers not just how much people play, but how they play. As richer forms of play data become available through industry-academic collaborations, researchers should move beyond the playtime paradigm to develop more sophisticated models of player experience and its relationship to psychological outcomes.


# References

<!-- References will auto-populate in the refs div below -->

# Appendix

## Appendix 1a: Visual Assessment of Cluster Tendency (VAT)

```{r appendix_vat_plots}
#| label: appendix_vat_plots
#| fig.width: 10
#| fig.height: 8
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Display VAT plots individually
print(dist_plot_real)
print(dist_plot_random)
```

## Appendix 1b: PCA Visualizations for Cluster Tendency

```{r appendix_pca_plots}
#| label: appendix_pca_plots
#| fig.width: 10
#| fig.height: 8
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Display PCA plots individually
print(pca_plot_real)
print(pca_plot_random)
```

## Appendix 2: Cluster Validation Plots

```{r appendix_cluster_validation_plots}
#| label: appendix_cluster_validation_plots
#| fig.width: 10
#| fig.height: 6
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Display the cluster validation plots that were created earlier
print(combined_pam_plots)

# Also display individual plots for better clarity
cat("\n### Silhouette Analysis\n")
print(p_pam_silhouette)

cat("\n### Calinski-Harabasz Index\n")
print(p_pam_ch)
```

The plots above show the validation methods used to determine the optimal number of clusters. The Silhouette method (top left) measures how well observations fit within their assigned clusters compared to neighboring clusters, with higher values indicating better fit. The Calinski-Harabasz Index (top right) evaluates cluster validity based on the ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster variance, with higher values indicating better separation between clusters. Both methods indicated that k = 3 was the optimal number of clusters for our player data.

## Appendix 3: Heatmaps of Slope Differences

```{r slope-heatmaps}
#| label: slope-heatmaps
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig.width: 10
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.cap: "Heatmaps of pairwise differences in simple slopes across player types"

# Create heatmap of slope differences for subjective well-being
slope_diff_matrix_wellbeing <- matrix(0, nrow = length(player_types), ncol = length(player_types))
p_matrix_wellbeing <- matrix(1, nrow = length(player_types), ncol = length(player_types))

for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
  for (j in 1:length(player_types)) {
    if (i != j) {
      # Find the correct pair in the pairwise comparison data
      if (i < j) {
        pair_row <- wellbeing_pairs[wellbeing_pairs$player_type1 == player_types[i] & 
                               wellbeing_pairs$player_type2 == player_types[j], ]
        slope_diff_matrix_wellbeing[i, j] <- pair_row$difference
        p_matrix_wellbeing[i, j] <- as.numeric(ifelse(pair_row$p.value == "< 0.001", 0.001, pair_row$p.value))
      } else {
        pair_row <- wellbeing_pairs[wellbeing_pairs$player_type1 == player_types[j] & 
                               wellbeing_pairs$player_type2 == player_types[i], ]
        slope_diff_matrix_wellbeing[i, j] <- -pair_row$difference  # Negate the difference
        p_matrix_wellbeing[i, j] <- as.numeric(ifelse(pair_row$p.value == "< 0.001", 0.001, pair_row$p.value))
      }
    }
  }
}

# Create a data frame for the heatmap
heatmap_data_wellbeing <- expand.grid(PlayerType1 = player_types, PlayerType2 = player_types)
heatmap_data_wellbeing$Difference <- as.vector(slope_diff_matrix_wellbeing)
heatmap_data_wellbeing$Significant <- as.vector(p_matrix_wellbeing) < 0.05

# Create the heatmap
heat_wellbeing <- ggplot(heatmap_data_wellbeing, aes(x = PlayerType1, y = PlayerType2, fill = Difference)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(PlayerType1 != PlayerType2, 
                              sprintf("%.3f%s", Difference, 
                                     ifelse(Significant, "*", "")), ""))) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Heatmap of Slope Differences for Game-Specific Well-being",
    subtitle = "Positive values indicate row player type has stronger slope than column player type",
    x = "Player Type",
    y = "Player Type",
    fill = "Difference"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

heat_wellbeing

# Create heatmap of slope differences for enjoyment model
slope_diff_matrix_enjoyment <- matrix(0, nrow = length(player_types), ncol = length(player_types))
p_matrix_enjoyment <- matrix(1, nrow = length(player_types), ncol = length(player_types))

for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
  for (j in 1:length(player_types)) {
    if (i != j) {
      # Find the correct pair in the pairwise comparison data
      if (i < j) {
        pair_row <- enjoyment_pairs[enjoyment_pairs$player_type1 == player_types[i] & 
                                   enjoyment_pairs$player_type2 == player_types[j], ]
        slope_diff_matrix_enjoyment[i, j] <- pair_row$difference
        p_matrix_enjoyment[i, j] <- as.numeric(ifelse(pair_row$p.value == "< 0.001", 0.001, pair_row$p.value))
      } else {
        pair_row <- enjoyment_pairs[enjoyment_pairs$player_type1 == player_types[j] & 
                                   enjoyment_pairs$player_type2 == player_types[i], ]
        slope_diff_matrix_enjoyment[i, j] <- -pair_row$difference  # Negate the difference
        p_matrix_enjoyment[i, j] <- as.numeric(ifelse(pair_row$p.value == "< 0.001", 0.001, pair_row$p.value))
      }
    }
  }
}

# Create a data frame for the heatmap
heatmap_data_enjoyment <- expand.grid(PlayerType1 = player_types, PlayerType2 = player_types)
heatmap_data_enjoyment$Difference <- as.vector(slope_diff_matrix_enjoyment)
heatmap_data_enjoyment$Significant <- as.vector(p_matrix_enjoyment) < 0.05

# Create the heatmap
heat_enjoyment <- ggplot(heatmap_data_enjoyment, aes(x = PlayerType1, y = PlayerType2, fill = Difference)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(PlayerType1 != PlayerType2, 
                              sprintf("%.3f%s", Difference, 
                                     ifelse(Significant, "*", "")), ""))) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Heatmap of Slope Differences for Intrinsic Enjoyment",
    subtitle = "Positive values indicate row player type has stronger slope than column player type",
    x = "Player Type",
    y = "Player Type",
    fill = "Difference"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

heat_enjoyment
```
